what_it_does: Stores a proposed operator-memory rule/policy entry that forbids routing around a slow or broken engine/model/option and requires fixing it instead.
observations: The file contains a single MEMORY dictionary with no executable code.
The rule is phrased as an absolute prohibition on suggesting alternatives.
Status is 'proposed' and noted as awaiting Tim's confirmation.
It is labeled as migrated from harness notes (B5 batch 1).
Evidence is a direct quote attributed to Tim on 2026-06-06 with an 'absolute' tag.
Scope is broad: triggered whenever anything underperforms or breaks and an alternative exists.
standouts: The absolute 'fix, never route around' rule.
The evidence is a direct operator quote rather than a technical rationale.
conventions: Follows an operator_memory schema with id, rule, why, evidence, scope, and status.
Docstring includes a migration provenance note.
concerns: Status is 'proposed', so the rule is not yet confirmed.
No mechanism is shown for how to enforce or apply the rule.
The scope is very broad and subjective ('anything underperforms or breaks').
The 'why' rests on a single quoted statement without broader context.
notes: A pending policy note that mandates fixing broken or slow engines rather than bypassing them, backed by a direct quote from Tim.
questions: How is this rule enforced in practice?
What does 'fixed' mean for a model, engine, or option?
Who confirms the proposed status and when?
What are the 'B5 batch 1 harness notes'?
What is the full operator_memory schema?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: it records a proposed rule and its rationale.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to encode a human operator principle into a reusable memory so that future assistance respects the directive to repair rather than replace failing components.
novelty: It is a behavioral policy substrate phrased as a negative absolute rule, supported by a dated direct quote and an explicit confirmation status.
contribution: Contributes a policy substrate that captures a fix-not-reroute principle and its operator rationale, which a fused system could retain as a behavioral guardrail.
summary_for_embedding: operator_memory/make_each_thing_work.py defines a proposed MEMORY rule that prohibits suggesting alternative engines, models, or options when one is slow or broken and requires fixing it instead, citing Tim's 2026-06-06 statement.
intention_for_embedding: To encode Tim's directive that every acquired engine must be tested and repaired rather than routed around into a reusable operator-memory policy.
